coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of novel carbon dots/cerium oxide nanocomposites for highly sensitive electrochemical detection of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Thakur, Neelam
Sharma, Vinit
Singh, Th. Abhishek
Pabbathi, Ashok
Das, Joydeep
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, carbon dots/cerium oxide (CDs/CeO2) nanocomposites-modified screen printed carbon electrode, SPCE (CDs/CeO2/SPCE) was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of doxorubicin (DOX), an anticancer drug. Here, firstly we have synthesized cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) from ammonium cerium nitrate and urea by simple refluxing, and then CDs were synthesized by using taurine (an essential amino acid) via thermal decomposition method. After that CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites have been synthesized with different wt% of CDs (0.5–5 wt%) via a facile hydrothermal method. The synthesized nanocomposites exhibited higher efficiency towards the electrochemical detection of DOX as compared to bare CeO2NPs and CDs by promoting electron transfer reaction at SPCE surface with increasing amount of CDs. The 5 wt% nanocomposite (CDs-5.0/CeO2) showed the highest oxidation response towards DOX (20 μM) at an optimized pH of 5. The cyclic voltammograms revealed that CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensing system exhibited a linear response (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.98) between oxidation peak current and DOX concentration in the range of 0.2–20 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.09 μM. Moreover, the modified CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensor exhibited superior selectivity towards DOX in the presence of common interferents. This work demonstrated that the novel CDs/CeO2/SPCE sensor can be effectively applied to detect DOX in pharmaceutical samples or in biological fluids.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of novel carbon dots/cerium oxide nanocomposites for highly sensitive electrochemical detection of doxorubicin

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, carbon dots/cerium oxide (CDs/CeO2) nanocomposites-modified screen printed carbon electrode, SPCE (CDs/CeO2/SPCE) was developed for the highly sensitive and selective detection of doxorubicin (DOX), an anticancer drug. Here, firstly we have synthesized cerium oxide nanoparticles (CeO2NPs) from ammonium cerium nitrate and urea by simple refluxing, and then CDs were synthesized by using taurine (an essential amino acid) via thermal decomposition method. After that CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites have been synthesized with different wt% of CDs (0.5–5 wt%) via a facile hydrothermal method. The synthesized nanocomposites exhibited higher efficiency towards the electrochemical detection of DOX as compared to bare CeO2NPs and CDs by promoting electron transfer reaction at SPCE surface with increasing amount of CDs. The 5 wt% nanocomposite (CDs-5.0/CeO2) showed the highest oxidation response towards DOX (20 μM) at an optimized pH of 5. The cyclic voltammograms revealed that CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensing system exhibited a linear response (correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.98) between oxidation peak current and DOX concentration in the range of 0.2–20 μM, with a low detection limit of 0.09 μM. Moreover, the modified CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensor exhibited superior selectivity towards DOX in the presence of common interferents. This work demonstrated that the novel CDs/CeO2/SPCE sensor can be effectively applied to detect DOX in pharmaceutical samples or in biological fluids.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is one of the most fatal and deadly diseases that has become a significant threat to human health worldwide, and chemotherapy is still considered as the first choice for cancer treatment [1–3]. Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) is one of the most effective anticancer anthracycline antibiotics that has been extensively used in the treatment of various kinds of cancers [4–8], but the clinical use is restricted due to its potential side effects [9,10]. Therefore, it is necessary to detect the level of DOX in clinical treatments in order to enhance its therapeutic efficiency and to avoid any possible side effects to other vital organs.

So far, many analytical techniques have been used for the determination of DOX, including high-performance liquid chromatography [11], mass spectrometry [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], UV spectroscopy [14], fluorescence spectroscopy [15], and raman spectroscopy [16]. However, these analytical techniques have various limitations, such as low sensitivity, complicated preconcentration, time-consuming procedure, and high cost [10,17].

Due to these shortcomings, nowadays researchers are interested towards various electrochemical methods such as, potentiometry [18], amperometry [19], and voltammetry [20] for the detection of DOX because of their unique advantages, such as high sensitivity and specificity, simple and low cost, and rapidness [6,17]. Among these electrochemical techniques, voltammetric-techniques including square wave voltammetry (SWV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and cyclic voltammetry (CV) are most promising techniques that are used by several researchers for DOX sensing [8,17,21]. DOX has electroactive quinone and hydroquinone groups that can be reduced or oxidized via a given applied potential; therefore the electrochemical detection of DOX is considered as one of the most effective techniques [6]. Furthermore, screen-printed carbon electrode (SPCE) is better suited for electrochemical detection of analytes as it can be modified easily, as well as exhibit low background current and wide potential window. Besides, these electrodes shows various attractive properties such as, excellent conductivity, high chemical stability, good reproducibility, high repeatability, good connectivity, quick response, and require low power [22,23]. In electrochemical sensing, the rate of electron transfer becomes very slow when unmodified electrodes are used for the detection of DOX. So far, several modifier nanomaterials have been used by researchers (to chemically modify the surface of electrodes) in order to improve the sensitivity and electron transfer rate in electrochemical sensing process of DOX [17,24–26].

In recent years, metal/metal oxides NPs as well as carbon nanomaterials or their composites show remarkable properties such as high conductivity, excellent electrocatalytic ability, strong surface reactivity, large specific surface area, enhancement of response signal and improvement of overpotentials for the fabrication of electrochemical nanosensors [2]. Cerium oxide NPs (CeO2NPs) or nanoceria has gained huge attention in electrochemical sensing application due to its unique and impressive properties such as high electrochemical stability, high isoelectric point (~9.2), good electronic conductivity, wide band gap, and good biocompatibility which significantly facilitates the speed of electron transfer [27]. Moreover, it possesses multiple oxygen vacancies, abundant active site, oxygen transfer ability, large surface area, switchable redox reactivity, enzyme mimetic properties and excellent catalytic activity due to which it has been considered as an ideal nanomaterial for electrochemical sensors [28–30]. These useful properties of CeO2NPs are a consequence of their unique ability to switch between two oxidation states (Ce3+ ↔ Ce4+) which enhances its electrochemical property [29]. CeO2 based nanomaterials have been significantly used for electrochemical detection of various electroactive analytes such as dopamine, uric acid, hydrogen peroxide, glucose, paracetamol, xanthine, and hypoxanthine [29,31]. However, CeO2NPs have a natural tendency to form aggregates which results in decreased stability and lower catalytic activity. Therefore, appropriate supporting substances are required to anchor CeO2NPs in order to enhance its conductivity, dispersion and catalytic activity [28].

Among different carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are one of the most emerging candidates of carbon family which generally consists of a nanocrystalline core of sp2 hybridised carbon and an amorphous shell or surface containing various functional groups [32]. Recently, CDs have attracted tremendous interest of nanotechnologists due to their superior properties like ultra-small size (< 10 nm), optical stability, chemical stability, low toxicity, good water solubility, remarkable conductivity, significant ability to donate as well as accept electrons, easy surface modification and biocompatibility [7,32–34]. These outstanding properties make them promising candidates for the use in various fields such as biomedicine, bioimaging, photodynamic and photothermal therapy, catalysis and sensing [34]. Hence, CDs or their composites have been effectively used as a modifier in the development of electrochemical sensors, and to facilitate the electron transfer process between electrode and sensing interface, thereby enhancing the redox reaction processes [7,35,36].

In the present work, we reported the synthesis of novel CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites via hydrothermal method for electrochemical detection of DOX. To the best of our knowledge, such a combination has never been explored for the electrochemical sensing of DOX. The as-synthesized CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites were characterized by HRTEM, XRD, XPS, FTIR, and Raman spectroscopy. Furthermore, the electrochemical behaviour of DOX on the surface of CDs/CeO2 nanocomposite modified SPCE has been observed. This modified SPCE sensor exhibits significant electrochemical activity for highly sensitive detection of DOX.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, Missouri, USA). Ammonium cerium nitrate (98%), urea, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), taurine, acetone (99.5%), concentrated HCl, potassium ferricyanide, sucrose, ascorbic acid, folic acid were bought from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Glucose, sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) monobasic and disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) dibasic were purchased from Himedia (Mumbai, India). Pure double distilled water was utilized in all the experiments.

CeO2NPs were synthesized following the previously reported procedure of Das et al. [37]. Briefly, a mixture of ammonium cerium nitrate (2 g) and urea (2 g) were refluxed in 200 mL distilled water for about 5 h at 95 °C. To this reaction mixture, NaOH solution (1 M) was added dropwise for maintaining the pH at ~7.4. After 5 h, the reaction solution was allowed to cool down followed by centrifugation for 10 min at 4000 rpm to obtain pale white NPs. The obtained NPs were washed three times with distilled water in order to remove any impurity and then dried overnight in an oven at 50 °C.

CDs were synthesized by a simple and fast one-step thermal decomposition method using taurine as a sole carbon precursor. Here, a hot plate was used as a heating source which was preheated to its highest capacity (~ 300 °C). In brief, 250 mg of taurine was dissolved in 5 mL distilled water in a 50 mL glass beaker. After that, the beaker containing the reaction mixture was placed on the preheated hot plate. Within 5–6 min, the mixture started to decompose into a dark coloured viscous solution/paste. After 9–10 min, dark grey coloured CDs were formed which were collected by spatula. The aqueous suspension of as-synthesized CDs were further dialysed with a 3500 Da MWCO membrane for purification. After that, the resulting solution was further lyophilized by using an Allied Frost lyophilizer (New Delhi, India) to obtain pure dark grey coloured CDs in powder form.

CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites were synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method reported by Pei et al. [38]. Firstly, 1.0 g of CeO2NPs were dissolved into 5 mL distilled water. To this solution, different amounts (5, 10, 25, and 50 mg) of CDs were added. This mixture was then ultrasonicated for 30 min followed by continuous stirring for 2 h. After that, the reaction mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and heated at 100 °C for 3 h. After the completion of reaction, the solution was allowed to cool down at room temperature and finally, the product was separated through centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min. The final product was further washed with distilled water and then dried in a hot air oven for overnight at 50 °C. The as-prepared CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites were labeled as CDs-0.5/CeO2, CDs-1.0/CeO2, CDs-2.5/CeO2, and CDs-5.0/CeO2 which corresponded to 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 wt% of CDs respectively in the nanocomposites.

For modification of SPCE, firstly, the suspensions of all the synthesized nanomaterials were prepared by dispersing 5 mg of each nanomaterial in 2 mL acetone (containing nafion, as a binder) followed by 30 min of ultrasonication. After that, 30 μL of the dispersed nanomaterial suspension was carefully drop-casted onto the surface of SPCE and allowed to dry at room temperature for 10 min. The obtained modified SPCEs were named as CeO2/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-1.0/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-2.5/CeO2/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE which were further used for electrochemical experiments.

The CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites were characterized by HRTEM, XPS, XRD, FTIR, Raman, UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopic analysis. The shape and size were obtained through a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM; JEOL, JEM 2100 plus). The elemental composition was determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Nexsa base; Thermo fisher scientific). The crystalline nature was analyzed via an X-ray diffractometer (X' Pert Pro) of Panalytical, Netherlands. The presence of specific functional groups were analyzed through fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) instrument (L1600312; CA, USA) of Agilent Technologies within the scale range of 400–4000 cm−1. Raman spectra was obtained on Horiba Japan, Xplora Plus Raman spectrometer. UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of all the nanomaterials were taken by using Cary 3500 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent technologies) and Varioskan LUX, 3020-563 spectrofluorimeter (Thermo fisher scientific), respectively. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out on FRA-2 μAUTOLAB TYPE III which was equipped with a standard three-electrode system included, CDs/CeO2/SPCE as a working electrode, carbon as a counter electrode, and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. Moreover, 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was used as a supporting electrolyte in all the electrochemical experiments. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique has been used for the determination of DOX through modified CDs/CeO2/SPCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CeO2, CDs, and CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In the current study, firstly we have prepared CeO2NPs and CDs, and then they were combined to fabricate a novel CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites (having different wt% of CDs) to investigate their electrochemical sensing ability towards DOX (Fig. 1 ).

The morphology and size of the prepared CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite was analyzed through HRTEM analysis (Fig. 2a), which showed nearly spherical shape with an average size of about 50–60 nm. XPS analysis was performed to attain information regarding the elemental composition of the synthesized CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite. The full spectrum analysis of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite showed the co-existence of cerium (Ce 3d), oxygen (O 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), carbon (C 1s), and sulfur (S 2p) as indicated by their respective binding energy peaks at 882.92 eV, 529.66 eV, 400.31 eV, 285.1 eV, and 169.21 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b). The XPS analysis thus confirmed the successful formation of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposites. In order to know about the crystal structure of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite, XRD analysis has been performed. The XRD pattern of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite is depicted in Fig. 2c which showed high-intensity peaks at 2θ values 28.460, 32.900, 47.540, 56.370, 69.090, and 76.930 corresponded to (111), (200), (220), (311), (400) and (331) crystal planes, respectively. All these diffraction peaks are related to the cubic fluorite structure of CeO2NPs [4,38]. No characteristic diffraction peak of CDs was found in the XRD pattern.

The chemical nature of all the synthesized nanomaterials was further investigated by FTIR analysis as depicted in Fig. 3a. The FTIR spectrum of pure CeO2NPs showed characteristic transmittance peaks at 481 and 620 cm−1 which were attributed to the stretching mode of vibration of CeO bond. The peaks at 3551, 3475, 3414 and 3236 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of –OH group, whereas the peak at 1617 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of adsorbed water molecules [39–41]. In case of pure CDs, transmittance peaks at 3465, 3415 and 3236 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibrations of –OH and -NH groups. Besides, the transmittance peaks at 1618 and 1511 cm−1 were assigned to asymmetrical and symmetrical NH2 deformation, while the peak at 1213 cm−1was assigned to CN stretching vibration. The peaks at 1106 and 1047 cm−1 were due to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of O=S=O. The transmittance peak at 740 cm−1 was observed due to CS stretching, whereas the peaks at 597 and 526 cm−1were assigned to asymmetrical and symmetrical SO3 deformation [42–45]. The infrared spectra of all the synthesized CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites exhibited almost similar characteristic peaks as that of pure CeO2NPs. The presence of specific functional groups on CDs/CeO2 nanocomposite has been further confirmed via Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectra of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite clearly showed the characteristic peaks of both CeO2 and CDs (Fig. 3b). The very intense peak at 461 cm−1 corresponded to CeO2 due to F2g vibration mode of oxygen atoms around the cerium cation, whereas the less intense peaks at 1345 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 represented D band and G band of CDs, respectively [46]. The D and G bands are associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds (sp3 defects), and vibrations of sp2 carbon atoms (E2g mode) of graphite respectively [47]. Furthermore, the appearance of two additional peaks at 1240 and 1447 cm−1 (labeled as D1 and D2) might be due to S-OH/SO3H, and S=O/S-O edge functional groups, respectively [48,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the synthesized nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of all the synthesized nanomaterials were investigated via both fluorescence and UV–Vis spectroscopy. Fig. 4a showed excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of pure CDs, i.e. the fluorescence emission shifted towards longer wavelengths (red-shift) with increasing the excitation wavelengths. In addition, the fluorescence emission of pure CDs also varied in intensity when excited from 320 to 460 nm and the strongest fluorescence intensity peak was observed near 430 nm after excitation at 340 nm. The fluorescence property of as-prepared CDs is due to the presence of sp2 conjugated carbon atoms as well as various carbon-related dangling bonds (defect states). Besides, the excitation-dependent (red-shifted) fluorescence emission of CDs might be attributed to the presence of oxygen content or different functional groups (such as SO, and CN) on the surface of CDs. It has been reported that the number of surface defects (capture centres for excitons) increased with increasing oxygen content which consequently results in red-shifted emission [50,51]. We have further analyzed the fluorescence spectra of all the synthesized nanomaterials at an excited wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 4b). Pure CeO2NPs as well as the synthesized nanocomposites (0.5–5 wt% CDs) did not exhibit any emission. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched due to electron or energy transfer of photoexcited CDs to Ce within the nanocomposites. The optical properties of pure CeO2NPs, pure CDs, and all the CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites were further evaluated through UV–Vis spectroscopic analysis as shown in Fig. 4c. The UV–Vis spectra of CDs exhibited an absorption peak at ~270 nm which might be attributed to π–π* transition of aromatic CC or CN sp2 domains of CDs [52,53]. Besides, CeO2NPs showed a strong absorption band at around 335 nm [54]. However, all the synthesized nanocomposites (CDs-0.5/CeO2, CDs-1.0/CeO2, CDs-2.5/CeO2, and CDs-5.0/CeO2) exhibited strong characteristic absorption band at ~310–325 nm with subsequent increase in the absorption intensity as compared to pure CeO2NPs. Furthermore, the most significant increase in the absorption intensity has been observed for CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to characterize each modified SPCE, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed by using 1 mM potassium ferricyanide, K3[Fe(CN)6] solution as a redox probe. For these electrochemical experiments, 60 μL of K3[Fe(CN)6] solution was dropped out onto the surface of each modified SPCE. As shown in Fig. 5a, cyclic voltammograms of all the modified SPCEs (CeO2/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-1.0/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-2.5/CeO2/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE) exhibited well-defined redox peaks at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and potential range from −0.6 to 0.6 V. However, among all the modified SPCEs, CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE showed significantly higher peak current which could be attributed to its fast electron transfer kinetics and large electro-active surface area [20]. To confirm this, effective surface area of all the modified electrodes has been calculated by using Randles-Sevcik equation [55,56]:

where Ip is peak current (A), n is the number of transferred electrons (n = 1), A is effective surface area (cm2), D is diffusion coefficient (for K3[Fe(CN)6] with value7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s), ν is the scan rate (V/s), C is concentration of K3[Fe(CN)6] (10−6 mol/cm3). The values of effective surface area, A were calculated to be 0.033, 0.035, 0.037, 0.04, 0.046, and 0.05 cm2 for CeO2/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-1.0/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-2.5/CeO2/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE, respectively (Table 1 ). These results clearly indicated that the electrode modified with CDs-5.0/CeO2 possesses larger reaction area as compared to other modified electrodes.

Moreover, EIS experiments were also performed to evaluate the charge transfer resistance of all the modified electrodes. As evident from Fig. 5b, Nyquist curve of CDs-5.0/CeO2 modified SPCE showed minimum arc radius as compared to other modified SPCEs (CeO2/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-1.0/CeO2/SPCE, and CDs-2.5/CeO2/SPCE). This indicates that, at CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE surface a very fast electron transfer reaction occurs which subsequently results in its higher electrical conductivity [8,57]. Hence, all these results demonstrated that CDs-5.0/CeO2 modified SPCE exhibited superior electrochemical performance as compared to other modified SPCEs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behaviours of DOX at different modified SPCEs
ce:section.ce:para: Cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique was used for investigating the electrochemical behaviour of DOX (20 μM) at different modified SPCEs. The solutions for modifying SPCE were prepared by suspending 5 mg of the synthesized nanomaterials in 2 mL of acetone containing nafion (as a binder) followed by ultrasonication for 30 min. Fig. 6a shows cyclic voltammograms of Bare/SPCE, CeO2/SPCE, CDs/SPCE, CDs-0.5/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-1.0/CeO2/SPCE, CDs-2.5/CeO2/SPCE, and CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE in the presence of 20 μM DOX in 0.1 (M) PBS (pH 5) at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. It has been observed from Fig. 6a that DOX did not show any redox peak with Bare/SPCE whereas, exhibits a pair of well-defined redox peaks with all the modified SPCEs within the potential range of −0.6 to 0.6 V. These results clearly indicate that all the modified electrodes facilitate the electron transfer rate of DOX and thus shows an efficient electrocatalytic response towards the redox reaction of DOX. Besides, among different modified electrodes, the SPCEs modified with CDs/CeO2 composites display superior peak current as compared to SPCEs modified with bare CeO2 or CDs. This might be due to the combined effect of both CeO2 and CDs present in the modified SPCEs, which significantly increase the active sites and surface area for electron transfer as well as efficiently promote conductivity [17]. Another reason may be strong interaction between DOX and CDs/CeO2 modified SPCEs due to significant π–π stacking interaction between aromatic DOX and CDs [58] resulting in the efficient attachment of DOX on SPCEs. Further, the increase in the concentration of CDs (0.5–5 wt%) within the nanocomposite results in an increase in the electrocatalytic activity of the modified SPCEs towards DOX oxidation. The oxidation peak current of DOX on CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE appeared at significantly higher value in comparison with other CDs/CeO2 modified SPCEs. These results clearly demonstrate that CDs play a very significant role in promoting the electrocatalytic activity of SPCEs. This might be due to the presence of sp2 hybridised carbon rich surface that assist in electron transfer and surface attachment of DOX. Since, CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE showed highest electrochemical signal response towards DOX, therefore this modified electrode has been used for all the subsequent electrochemical sensing experiments. The electrochemical oxidation mechanism of DOX at modified SPCE is depicted in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentrations of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of the amount of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite used for the modification of SPCE was done through CV technique in 20 μM DOX solution. Seven different suspensions of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposites were prepared by taking 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8 mg of nanocomposite in 2 mL of acetone containing nafion followed by ultrasonication (30 min). The redox peak current of DOX was significantly influenced by the amount of CDs-5.0/CeO2 casted on SPCE. It has been observed that the oxidation peak current of DOX was increased with increasing the concentration of CDs-5.0/CeO2. The peak current of DOX reached the maximum value when the concentration of CDs-5.0/CeO2 was 5 mg/2 mL. However, further increase in the concentration dramatically decreased the oxidation peak current (Fig. 6b) probably due to the increase in thickness of CDs-5.0/CeO2 coating on the electrode surface. Therefore, 5 mg of CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite in 2 mL acetone was chosen as optimal concentration to modify SPCE for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of supporting electrolyte
ce:section.ce:para: The pH of the supporting electrolyte solution affects the peak current as well as the peak potential of DOX. The influence of pH of the supporting electrolyte (0.1 M PBS) on the electrochemical behaviour of DOX (20 μM) using CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE was investigated within the pH range of 3 to 6 at the scan rate of 50 mVs−1 (Fig. 7a). It has been observed that the anodic peak current (Ipa) of DOX reached the maximum value at pH = 5 (Fig. 7b). Hence, pH = 5 was chosen as the optimum pH condition for further electrochemical measurements. Moreover, it has been observed that the anodic peak potential (Epa) of DOX shifted towards less positive values with increase in pH from 3 to 6 (Fig. 7c). These results suggested that protons are involved in the oxidation process of DOX on CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE. The pH dependence of Epa can be expressed by the following linear regression equation: Epa (V) = −0.051pH + 0.487 with a correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.99. The calculated slope of 0.051 V pH −1 was close to the theoretical value 0.059 V pH −1 which indicated that an equal number of protons and electrons were involved in the electrochemical oxidation process of DOX at CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE according to the Nernst equation [6,56,59]. It has also been illustrated in Fig. 1 that DOX can be oxidized via two protons and two electrons.

The influence of scan rate has also been analyzed in the range of 50–200 mV/s by CV method in order to elucidate the electro-oxidation mechanism of DOX (20 μM) at the surface of CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE in the presence of 0.1 M PBS (pH 5) (Fig. 8a). It has been observed from Fig. 8a that with increasing the scan rate, the oxidation peak current also increased. Moreover, a linear relationship has been obtained between logarithm of anodic peak current (log Ipa) and logarithm of scan rate (log ν) with the linear regression equation: log Ipa = 0.31544 log ν + 0.32151 (R2 = 0.99) (Fig. 8b). The calculated slope of 0.31 revealed that the electrochemical reaction of DOX is mainly diffusion-controlled. A slope close to 1.0 is expected for adsorption-controlled electrode process, and close to 0.5 for diffusion-controlled process [6,25,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different concentrations of DOX at CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical response of DOX has been investigated by varying its concentration (0.2–20 μM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 5) using CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1 and potential range of −0.6 to 0.6 V. Fig. 9a showed cyclic voltammograms of different concentration of DOX at CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE surface. It has been observed that the oxidation peak current response of DOX increased significantly with increasing its concentration on CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of limit of detection (LOD) for DOX concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The LOD of DOX was calculated from the calibration curve of concentration vs anodic peak current (Ipa) as depicted in Fig. 9b. From this calibration plot, a good linear relationship has been observed between Ipa and the concentration of DOX within the range 0.2–20 μM with a correlation coefficient (R2) = 0.98. The LOD of DOX determination was calculated by using the formula as given below:

LOD = 3 s/m; where “s” refers to the standard deviation of the anodic peak current and “m” refers to the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of DOX was found to be 0.09 μM by using the linear regression equation y = 1.39456x + 1.7734 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Hence, we proposed that the as-prepared CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE showed a promising potential for electrochemical detection of DOX with a suitable linear range and low LOD. The comparison of the results for DOX determination by using different modified electrodes has been summarized in Table 2 . The comparative data suggested that the obtained LOD value with our CDs-5.0/CeO2 modified SPCE is comparable or even better than the values as reported in previous literature [8,20,21,59,61–64]. These results revealed that CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite can be effectively used as an electrode modifier to develop electroanalytical methods for the detection of DOX in real or commercial samples. However, we restricted our studies with low CD content, i.e. upto only 5 wt% of CD in the nanocomposites because higher amount of CDs in the nanocomposites may cover the active surface of the catalysts (CeO2), thereby decreasing the electrocatalytic efficiency of the nanocomposite [38].

The selectivity of CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensor towards DOX detection was examined by using CV technique at a scan rate of 50 mVs−1. The influence of several interfering species like ascorbic acid (100 μM), folic acid (100 μM), urea (100 μM), sucrose (100 μM), and glucose (100 μM) on the oxidation signal of DOX (10 μM) at pH 5 was investigated at the surface of CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE. As shown in Fig. 10 , no substantial change in the peak current intensity of DOX (10 μM) has been observed even in the presence of 10-fold higher concentration of all the interfering species. These results demonstrated that CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensor exhibits good selectivity to determine DOX.

Here, we have successfully synthesized a novel CDs/CeO2 nanocomposite via a facile hydrothermal method for highly sensitive electrochemical detection of DOX on the surface of SPCE. The combination of CeO2NPs and CDs as a modifier on SPCE displayed significantly higher electrochemical response towards DOX determination as compared to bare CeO2NPs and CDs. Among various synthesized CDs/CeO2 nanocomposites, SPCE modified with CDs-5.0/CeO2 nanocomposite has shown the highest oxidation peak current towards the oxidation of DOX at pH 5 with a detection limit of 0.09 μM. Hence, the outcomes of our study demonstrate that CDs-5.0/CeO2/SPCE sensing system may be applicable for highly selective and sensitive detection of DOX in pharmaceutical or real samples in future.
